+  long lastconnect_id; /* The last connection, -1 if undefined */
   struct dynbuf headerb; /* buffer to store headers in */
 
   char *buffer; /* download buffer */

commit 0c543e3f5b3881d515d6235f152aacaaaf3aba72
Author:     Yann Ylavic <ylavic@apache.org>
AuthorDate: Fri Jul 24 09:35:25 2020 +0000
Commit:     Yann Ylavic <ylavic@apache.org>
CommitDate: Fri Jul 24 09:35:25 2020 +0000

    Merge r1880205, r1880214 from trunk:
    
    mod_proxy_uwsgi: Error out on HTTP header larger than 16K
    
    The uwsgi protocol does not let us serialize more than 16K of HTTP header,
    so fail early with 500 if it happens.
    
    
    Follow up to r1880205, APLOGN

content.hunk_text:
@@ -1,6 +1,9 @@
                                                          -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
 Changes with Apache 2.4.44
 
+  *) mod_proxy_uwsgi: Error out on HTTP header larger than 16K (hard
+     protocol limit).  [Yann Ylavic]
+
   *) mod_http2: 
      Fixes <https://github.com/icing/mod_h2/issues/200>: 
      "LimitRequestFields 0" now disables the limit, as documented.

Commit:     Yann Ylavic <ylavic@apache.org>
CommitDate: Thu Jul 23 14:09:50 2020 +0000

    mod_proxy_uwsgi: Error out on HTTP header larger than 16K
    
    The uwsgi protocol does not let us serialize more than 16K of HTTP header,
    so fail early with 500 if it happens.
    
    
    
    git-svn-id: https://svn.apache.org/repos/asf/httpd/httpd/trunk@1880205

    reclaim also includes it being unmapped from KVM's memslots).  Due to lack
    of coherency for SEV encrypted memory,

commit 105cd17a866017b45f3c45901b394c711c97bf40
Author:     Taehee Yoo <ap420073@gmail.com>
AuthorDate: Mon Jul 5 15:38:07 2021 +0000
Commit:     David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
CommitDate: Tue Jul 6 10:36:59 2021 -0700

    bonding: fix null dereference in bond_ipsec_add_sa()
    
    If bond doesn't have real device, bond->curr_active_slave is null.
    But bond_ipsec_add_sa() dereferences bond->curr_active_slave without
    null checking.
    So, null-ptr-deref would occur.
    
    Tes

    If bond doesn't have real device, bond->curr_active_slave is null.
    But bond_ipsec_add_sa() dereferences bond->curr_active_slave without
    null checking.
    So, null-ptr-deref would occur.
    
    Tes

content.description: The `ff_dnn_load_model_native` function did not validate the `operand_index` against the total number of `network->operands_num`, leading to potential out-of-bounds array access.
The `ff_dnn_load_model_native` function now passes `network->operands_num` to `pf_load` functions, enabling subsequent operand index validation.
A guard condition was added in `ff_dnn_load_model_native` to check if `operand_index` is greater than or equal to `network->operands_num`, preventing out-of-bounds access.
The condition `if (operand_index >= network->operands_num)` was added to prevent out-of-bounds access.

content.summary: The vulnerability allows out-of-bounds access due to insufficient validation of operand indices when loading DNN layers from a model file. The fix introduces checks to ensure operand indices are within valid bounds.
content.mechanism: The 'ff_dnn_load_model_native' function and various 'dnn_load_layer_*' functions did not properly validate operand indices read from the model file against the total number of operands. This allowed an attacker to craft a malicious model file with out-of-bounds operand indices, leading to potential out-of-bounds memory access.

content.pattern: -  struct connectdata *lastconnect; /* The last connection, NULL if undefined */\n+  long lastconnect_id; /* The last connection, -1 if undefined */

content.description: The 'data->state.lastconnect' pointer in 'struct UrlState' could become a dangling pointer if the pointed-to 'struct connectdata' was freed, leading to a Use-After-Free when subsequently accessed, for instance in Curl_getconnectinfo.

The 'lastconnect' pointer was replaced with 'lastconnect_id' (a long integer). This ID is now used to look up connections in the cache, ensuring that only valid, existing connections are referenced and preventing the use of freed memory.

content.summary: libcurl suffered from a Use-After-Free vulnerability due to a dangling pointer to the last connection. When a Curl_easy handle was removed from a multi handle, the associated connection could be closed and freed, but the Curl_easy handle's 'lastconnect' pointer was not reset. Subsequent calls to Curl_getconnectinfo would then attempt to dereference this freed pointer.

diff --git a/CHANGES b/CHANGES
index bbd6d1ec64..87fbed9786 100644
--- a/CHANGES
+++ b/CHANGES
@@ -1,83 +1,86 @@
                                                          -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
 Changes with Apache 2.5.1
 
+  *) mod_proxy_uwsgi: Error out on HTTP header larger than 16K (hard
+     protocol limit).  [Yann Ylavic]
+
   *) mod_dav: Some DAV extensions, like CalDAV, specify both document
      elements and property elements that need to be taken into account
      when generating a property. The document element and property element
      are made available in the dav_liveprop_elem structure under the
      DAV_PROP_ELEMENT key in the resource pool. [Graham Leggett]
 
   *) mod_dav: Add utility functions dav_validate_root_ns(),
      dav_find_child_ns(), dav_find_next_ns(), dav_find_attr_ns() and
      dav_find_attr() so that other modules get to play too.
      [Graham Leggett]
 
   *) mod_http2: 
      Fixes <https://github.com/icing/mod_h2/issues/200>: 
      "LimitRequestFields 0" now disables the limit, as documented.
      Fixes <https://github.com/icing/mod_h2/issues/201>: 
      Do not count repeated headers with same name against the field
      count limit. The are merged internally, as if sent in a single HTTP/1 line.
      [Stefan Eissing]
 
   *) mod_http2: Avoid segfaults in case of handling certain responses for
      already aborted connections.  [Stefan Eissing, Ruediger Pluem]
 
   *) core: Remove support for the Content-MD5 header, removed in RFC7231.
      Functions ap_md5digest() and ap_md5contextT

0c543e3f5b Merge r1880205, r1880214 from trunk:
313d5ee40f Merge r1859371, r1859422 from trunk:
13f5446d83 Merge r1839303, r1843290 from trunk:
99c59e0981 mod_proxy_uwsgi: Add in UWSGI proxy (sub)module trunk patch: http://svn.apache.org/r1810358              http://svn.apache.org/r1810362              http://svn.apache.org/r1810363              http://svn.apache.org/r1810365              http://svn.apache.org/r1810447              http://svn.apache.org/r1816919              http://svn.apache.org/r1816922              http://svn.apache.org/r1818013              http://svn.apache.org/r1818280 +1: jim, icing, minfrin icing: tested on ubuntu 16.04 with a simple uwsgi python app.

content.description: The `pktsize` variable was defined as `apr_uint16_t`, limiting the maximum packet size to 65535 bytes (APR_UINT16_MAX), which could lead to an integer overflow if the calculated header length (`headerlen - 4`) exceeded this value.

Prior to the fix, no explicit check was performed to limit the HTTP header size within the uwsgi protocol's specified 16KB limit. This allowed potentially oversized headers to be processed, leading to an overflow when `pktsize` was later used in `buf[1]` and `buf[2]` as a 16-bit value.
The `pktsize` variable's type was changed from `apr_uint16_t` to `apr_size_t`, allowing it to correctly represent HTTP header sizes larger than 65535 bytes, preventing integer overflow.

An explicit check was added to the `uwsgi_send_headers` function, returning `HTTP_INTERNAL_SERVER_ERROR` if the calculated `pktsize` (representing the header length) exceeds `APR_UINT16_MAX`. This ensures adherence to the uwsgi protocol's header size limit and prevents attempts to send overly large headers.

content.summary: An integer overflow vulnerability in `mod_proxy_uwsgi` allowed HTTP headers exceeding the uwsgi protocol's implicit 16KB limit to cause incorrect packet size calculations. The fix involves changing the `pktsize` variable type to `apr_size_t` and adding an explicit size validation check against `APR_UINT16_MAX` to prevent overflow and enforce the protocol limit, returning a 500 error if exceeded.

content.stdout_excerpt:
commit 683412ccf61294d727ead4a73d97397396e69a6b
Author: Mingwei Zhang <mizhang@google.com>
Date:   Thu Apr 21 03:14:07 2022 +0000

    KVM: SEV: add cache flush to solve SEV cache incoherency issues
    
    Flush the CPU caches when memory is reclaimed from an SEV guest (where
    reclaim also includes it being unmapped from KVM's memslots).  Due to lack
    of coherency for SEV encrypted memory, failure to flush results in silent
    data corruption if userspace is malicious/broken and doesn't ensure SEV
    guest memory is properly pinned and unpinned.
    
    Cache coherency is not enforced across the VM boundary in SEV (AMD APM
    vol.2 Section 15.34.7). Confidential cachelines, generated by confidential
    VM guests have to be explicitly flushed on the host side. If a memory page
    containing dirty confidential cachelines was released by VM and reallocated
    to another user, the cachelines may corrupt the new user at a later time.
    
    KVM takes a shortcut by assuming all confidential memory remain pinned
    until the end of VM lifetime. Therefore, KVM does not flush cache at
    mmu_notifier invalidation events. Because of this incorrect assumption and
    the lack of cache flushing, malicous userspace can crash the host kernel:
    creating a malicious VM and continuously allocates/releases unpinned
    confidential memory pages when the VM is running.
    
    Add cache flush operations to mmu_notifier operations to ensure that any
    physical memory leaving the guest VM get flushed. In particular, hook
    mmu_notifier_invalidate_range_start and mmu_notifier_release events and
    flush cache accordingly. The hook after releasing the mmu lock to avoid
    contention with other vCPUs.
    
    Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
    Suggested-by: Sean Christpherson <seanjc@google.com>
    Reported-by: Mingwei Zhang <mizhang@google.com>
    Signed-off-by: Mingwei Zhang <mizhang@google.com>
    Message-Id: <20220421031407.2516575-4-mizhang@google.com>
    Signed-off-by: Paolo Bonzini <pbonzini@redhat.com>

    containing dirty confidential cachelines was released by VM and reallocated
    to another user, the cachelines may corrupt the new user at a later time.
    
    KVM takes a shortcut by assuming all confidential memory remain pinned
    until the end of VM lifetime. Therefore, KVM does not flush cache at
    mmu_notifier invalidation events. Because of this incorrect assumption and
    the lack of cache flushing, malicous userspace can crash the host kernel:
    creating a malicious VM and continuously allocates/releases unpinned
    confidential memory pages when the VM is running.
    
    Add cache flush operations to mmu_notifier operations to ensure that any
    physical memory leaving the guest VM get flushed. In particular, hook
    mmu_notifier_invalidate_range_start and mmu_notifier_release events and
    flush cache accordingly. The hook after releasing the mmu lock to avoid
    contention with other vCPUs.

content.claim: git show --stat for fix commit 683412ccf61294d727ead4a73d97397396e69a6b
content.snippet:
commit 683412ccf61294d727ead4a73d97397396e69a6b
Author: Mingwei Zhang <mizhang@google.com>
Date:   Thu Apr 21 03:14:07 2022 +0000

    KVM: SEV: add cache flush to solve SEV cache incoherency issues
    
    Flush the CPU caches when memory is reclaimed from an SEV guest (where
    reclaim also includes it being unmapped from KVM's memslots).  Due to lack
    of coherency for SEV encrypted memory, failure to flush results in silent
    data corruption if userspace is malicious/broken and doesn't ensure SEV
    guest memory is properly pinned and unpinned.
    
    Cache coherency is not enforced across the VM boundary in SEV (AMD APM
    vol.2 Section 15.34.7). Confidential cachelines, generated by confidential
    VM guests have to be explicitly flushed on the host side. If a memory page
    containing dirty confidential cachelines was released by VM and reallocated
    to another user, the cachelines may corrupt the new user at a later time.
    
    KVM takes a shortcut by assuming all confidential memory remain pinned
    until the end of VM lifetime. Therefore, KVM does not flush cache at
    mmu_notifier invalidation events. Because of this incorrect assumption and
    the lack of cache flushing, malicous userspace can crash the host kernel:
    creating a malicious VM and continuously allocates/releases unpinned
    confidential memory pages when the VM is running.
    
    Add cache flush operations to mmu_notifier operations to ensure that any
    physical memory leaving the guest VM get flushed. In particular, hook
    mmu_notifier_invalidate_range_start and mmu_notifier_release events and
    flush cache accordingly. The hook after releasing the mmu lock to avoid
    contention with other vCPUs.
    
    Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
    Suggested-by: Sean Christpherson <seanjc@google.com>
    Reported-by: Mingwei Zhang <mizhang@google.com>
    Signed-off-by: Mingwei Zhang <mizhang@google.com>
    Message-Id: <20220421031407.2516575-4-mizhang@google.com>

commit 105cd17a866017b45f3c45901b394c711c97bf40
Author: Taehee Yoo <ap420073@gmail.com>
Date:   Mon Jul 5 15:38:07 2021 +0000

    bonding: fix null dereference in bond_ipsec_add_sa()
    
    If bond doesn't have real device, bond->curr_active_slave is null.
    But bond_ipsec_add_sa() dereferences bond->curr_active_slave without
    null checking.
    So, null-ptr-deref would occur.
    
    Test commands:
        ip link add bond0 type bond
        ip link set bond0 up
        ip x s add proto esp dst 14.1.1.1 src 15.1.1.1 spi \
    0x07 mode transport reqid 0x07 replay-window 32 aead 'rfc4106(gcm(aes))' \
    0x44434241343332312423222114131211f4f3f2f1 128 sel src 14.0.0.52/24 \
    dst 14.0.0.70/24 proto tcp offload dev bond0 dir in
    
    Splat looks like:
    KASAN: null-ptr-deref in range [0x0000000000000000-0x0000000000000007]
    CPU: 4 PID: 680 Comm: ip Not tainted 5.13.0-rc3+ #1168
    RIP: 0010:bond_ipsec_add_sa+0xc4/0x2e0 [bonding]

    knlGS:0000000000000000
    CS:  0010 DS: 0000 ES: 0000 CR0: 0000000080050033
    CR2: 000055c2530dbf40 CR3: 0000000103056004 CR4: 00000000003706e0
    DR0: 0000000000000000 DR1: 0000000000000000 DR2: 0000000000000000
    DR3: 0000000000000000 DR6: 00000000fffe0ff0 DR7: 0000000000000400
    Call Trace:
     xfrm_dev_state_add+0x2a9/0x770
     ? memcpy+0x38/0x60
     xfrm_add_sa+0x2278/0x3b10 [xfrm_user]

     ? sched_clock_cpu+0x18/0x170
     netlink_rcv_skb+0x121/0x350
     ? xfrm_user_state_lookup.constprop.39+0x320/0x320 [xfrm_user]
     ? netlink_ack+0x9d0/0x9d0
     ? netlink_deliver_tap+0x17c/0xa50
     xfrm_netlink_rcv+0x68/0x80 [xfrm_user]
     netlink_unicast+0x41c/0x610
     ? netlink_attachskb+0x710/0x710
     netlink_sendmsg+0x6b9/0xb70
    [ ...]
    
    Fixes: 18cb261afd7b ("bonding: support hardware encryption offload to slaves")
    Signed-off-by: Taehee Yoo <ap420073@gmail.com>
    Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>

    bonding: fix null dereference in bond_ipsec_add_sa()
    
    If bond doesn't have real device, bond->curr_active_slave is null.
    But bond_ipsec_add_sa() dereferences bond->curr_active_slave without
    null checking.
    So, null-ptr-deref would occur.
    
    Test commands:
        ip link add bond0 type bond
        ip link set bond0 up
        ip x s add proto esp dst 14.1.1.1 src 15.1.1.1 spi \
    0x07 mode transport reqid 0x07 replay-window 32 aead 'rfc4106(gcm(aes))' \
    0x44434241343332312423222114131211f4f3f2f1 128 sel src 14.0.0.52/24 \
    dst 14.0.0.70/24 proto tcp offload dev bond0 dir in

content.description: The code would dereference `bond->curr_active_slave` (assigned to `slave`) without a null check. If `bond->curr_active_slave` was null (e.g., when the bond doesn't have a real device), this would lead to a null pointer dereference when `xs->xso.real_dev = slave->dev;` is executed.

A null check `if (!slave)` was added after `slave = rcu_dereference(bond->curr_active_slave);`. If `slave` is null, the function now releases the RCU read lock and returns `-ENODEV`, preventing the null pointer dereference.

content.summary: The root cause of CVE-2022-0286 is a null pointer dereference in the `bond_ipsec_add_sa` function. The `bond->curr_active_slave` was dereferenced without proper null checking, leading to a crash if `bond` did not have a real device.

content.description: Variables `opj_image_t* image`, `opj_stream_t *l_stream`, `opj_codec_t* l_codec`, and `opj_codestream_index_t* cstr_index` were initialized to NULL only once at the beginning of the `main` function, outside the loop for processing multiple images. This could lead to a double-free if these pointers were freed due to an error during image processing and then subsequently freed again or used after being freed, particularly when processing a directory with a mix of valid and invalid images.

The variables `opj_image_t* image`, `opj_stream_t *l_stream`, `opj_codec_t* l_codec`, and `opj_codestream_index_t* cstr_index` are now initialized to `NULL` inside the image processing loop (`for (imageno = 0; imageno < num_images ; imageno++)`). This ensures that for each new image, these pointers are reset, preventing potential double-free issues or use-after-free conditions that could arise from re-using pointers to previously freed memory.

content.summary: The vulnerability is a double-free issue caused by variables related to image decoding (opj_image_t*, opj_stream_t*, opj_codec_t*, opj_codestream_index_t*) being initialized once outside a loop that processes multiple images. This leads to these pointers potentially holding freed memory addresses across loop iterations or on subsequent attempts to free them after an error, resulting in a double-free.
content.mechanism: Before the fix, `image`, `l_stream`, `l_codec`, and `cstr_index` were declared and initialized to `NULL` only once at the beginning of the `main` function. When processing multiple images in a directory, if an error occurred during the decompression of one image, these pointers might have been freed partially or fully. In subsequent loop iterations, or upon exiting the function, if these pointers were not reset to `NULL` or re-initialized, they could point to previously freed memory, leading to a double-free if the free operation was attempted again on the same memory address. The fix addresses this by moving the initialization of these variables to `NULL` inside the image processing loop, ensuring they are reset for each image, thereby preventing the use-after-free or double-free condition.

commit eb563247aef3e83dda7679c43f9649270462e5b1
Author: Benjamin Kaduk <kaduk@mit.edu>
Date:   Fri Apr 10 12:27:28 2020 -0700

    Fix NULL dereference in SSL_check_chain() for TLS 1.3
    
    In the tls1_check_sig_alg() helper function, we loop through the list of
    "signature_algorithms_cert" values received from the client and attempt
    to look up each one in turn in our internal table that maps wire
    codepoint to string-form name, digest and/or signature NID, etc., in
    order to compare the signature scheme from the peer's list against what
    is used to sign the certificates in the certificate chain we're
    checking.  Unfortunately, when the peer sends a value that we don't
    support, the lookup returns NULL, but we unconditionally dereference the
    lookup result for the comparison, leading to an application crash
    triggerable by an unauthenticated client.
    
    Since we will not be able to say anything about algorithms we don't
    recognize, treat NULL return from lookup as "does not match".
    
    We currently only apply the "signature_algorithm_cert" checks on TLS 1.3
    connections, so previous TLS versions are unaffected.  SSL_check_chain()
    is not called directly from libssl, but may be used by the application
    inside a callback (e.g., client_hello or cert callback) to verify that a
    candidate certificate chain will be acceptable to the client.
    
    CVE-2020-1967
    
    Reviewed-by: Matt Caswell <matt@openssl.org>

 ssl/t1_lib.c | 2 +-
 1 file changed, 1 insertion(+), 1 deletion(-)

eb563247ae Fix NULL dereference in SSL_check_chain() for TLS 1.3
e7ff223a20 Update copyright year
57225c99ef Check that ed25519 and ed448 are allowed by the security level
cc7c6eb813 Check that the default signature type is allowed
eed2c919fc Workaround for Windows-based GOST implementations

A NULL check for `sigalg` was added before dereferencing it, preventing the NULL pointer dereference.

 #include "qemu/log.h"
 #include "ui/pixel_ops.h"
 
 /*
  * NOTE:
  * This is 2D _acceleration_ and supposed to be fast. Therefore, don't try to
  * reinvent the wheel (unlikely to get better with a naive implementation than
  * existing libraries) and avoid (poorly) reimplementing gfx primitives.
  * That is unnecessary and would become a performance problem. Instead, try to
  * map to and reuse existing optimised facilities (e.g. pixman) wherever
  * possible.
  */
 
 static int ati_bpp_from_datatype(ATIVGAState *s)
 {
     switch (s->regs.dp_datatype & 0xf) {
     case 2:
         return 8;
     case 3:
     case 4:
         return 16;
     case 5:
         return 24;
     case 6:
         return 32;
     default:
         qemu_log_mask(LOG_UNIMP, "Unknown dst datatype %d\n",
                       s->regs.dp_datatype & 0xf);
         return 0;
     }
 }
 
 #define DEFAULT_CNTL (s->regs.dp_gui_master_cn

content.description: The `dst_x` and `dst_y` coordinates were defined as signed integers, which could lead to incorrect coordinate calculations or integer overflows/underflows, particularly for reverse blit operations controlled by a malicious guest.
There was no explicit check for `bpp` (bits per pixel) being zero, which could result in invalid calculations or crashes if `bpp` was zero.

There was no explicit check for `dst_stride` (destination pitch) being zero, potentially leading to division by zero errors or other issues when `pixman` functions are called with invalid stride values.

The `src_x` and `src_y` coordinates were defined as signed integers, which could lead to incorrect coordinate calculations or integer overflows/underflows when the source region is manipulated by a malicious guest.
There was no explicit check for `src_stride` (source pitch) being zero, potentially leading to division by zero errors or other issues when `pixman` functions are called with invalid stride values.

The `dst_x` and `dst_y` registers were updated incrementally without considering the `DST_X_LEFT_TO_RIGHT` and `DST_Y_TOP_TO_BOTTOM` flags, which could lead to incorrect coordinate updates for certain blit operations.
The `dst_x` and `dst_y` coordinates were changed to unsigned integers (`unsigned`), preventing negative coordinate calculations and potential integer overflows/underflows.
Added a check for `bpp` being zero, returning early if it is, preventing invalid `pixman` calls.
Added a check for `dst_stride` being zero, returning early if it is, preventing division by zero or other errors in `pixman` functions.
The `src_x` and `src_y` coordinates were changed to unsigned integers (`unsigned`), preventing negative coordinate calculations and potential integer overflows/underflows.
Added a check for `src_stride` being zero, returning early if it is, preventing division by zero or other errors in `pixman` functions.
The `dst_x` and `dst_y` registers are now updated conditionally based on the `DST_X_LEFT_TO_RIGHT` and `DST_Y_TOP_TO_BOTTOM` flags, ensuring correct coordinate progression for all blit directions.

content.summary: The vulnerability in QEMU's ati_2d_blt function (ATI VGA emulation) arises from the use of signed integers for destination and source coordinates (dst_x, dst_y, src_x, src_y) and the lack of validation for bit-per-pixel (bpp) and pitch values (dst_stride, src_stride). This could lead to integer overflows/underflows during coordinate calculations and invalid parameters passed to pixman functions, resulting in crashes due to out-of-bounds access or division by zero, especially when a malicious guest provides crafted input. Additionally, the update logic for destination coordinates was incorrect for certain blit operations.
content.mechanism: Integer overflow/underflow, missing input validation, incorrect coordinate calculation logic.

content.description: The NFS dissector did not detect and handle filesystem cycles when recursively snooping full names in `nfs_full_name_snoop`, leading to potential infinite recursion and resource exhaustion.

The NFS dissector now detects filesystem cycles and excessive recursion depth in `nfs_full_name_snoop` by tracking recursion depth and setting a `fs_cycle` flag, preventing infinite loops and resource exhaustion.
An expert information message (`ei_nfs_file_system_cycle`) is now added to the dissection tree when a filesystem cycle is detected, alerting the user to the potential issue.

content.summary: The NFS dissector in Wireshark was vulnerable to a denial of service due to unchecked recursion when processing Network File System (NFS) packets with cyclical file system structures. The fix introduces a mechanism to detect and prevent excessive recursion depth and filesystem cycles, mitigating resource exhaustion.
content.mechanism: Lack of recursion depth check during full name snooping in NFS dissector.
